comments.text: Gpt 5.5 pro found a partial result Let \(L=L(x)\) satisfy\[ L\log(2L)=o((\log x)^2). \]Then for all but \(o(x)\) integers \(n\le x\), the values\[ \varphi(n+1),\varphi(n+2),\ldots,\varphi(n+L) \]are pairwise distinct. In particular, this gives an affirmative answer to the problem for every fixed exponent \(c<2\), taking \(L=\lfloor(\log x)^c\rfloor\). It also gives, for example,\[ L=\frac{(\log x)^2}{(\log\log x)^{1+\varepsilon}} \]for any fixed \(\varepsilon>0\). The broad idea is to count bad starting points. If \(n\) is bad, then for some \(1\le i<j\le L\),\[ \varphi(n+i)=\varphi(n+j). \]Writing \(h=j-i\) and \(m=n+i\), this gives a shifted equal-totient equation\[ \varphi(m)=\varphi(m+h). \]Thus the number of bad starts is bounded by\[ \sum_{h<L}(L-h)P(x+L;h), \]where\[ P(X;h)=\#\{m\le X:\varphi(m)=\varphi(m+h)\}. \]The note uses the Graham--Holt--Pomerance / Pollack--Pomerance--Treviño decomposition (references in note)\[ P(X;h)=P_0(X;h)+P_1(X;h). \]The exceptional part \(P_1\) is exponentially small uniformly for polylogarithmic \(h\). The structured part satisfies\[ P_0(X;h)\ll c(h)\frac{X}{(\log X)^2}. \]The extra observation in the note is that the structured constants have enough average saving:\[ \sum_{h\le L} c(h)\ll \log L. \]Combining these gives\[ \begin{aligned} \#\{\text{bad }n\le x\} &\ll \frac{x}{(\log x)^2} \sum_{h<L}(L-h)c(h) + o(x) \\ &\ll x\frac{L\log L}{(\log x)^2}+o(x). \end{aligned} \]This is \(o(x)\) whenever\[ L\log L=o((\log x)^2). \]So this does not solve the full problem for arbitrary fixed \(c>0\), but it does give a rigorous almost-all result up to essentially the \((\log x)^2\) scale.
Thanks! Standard check found no issues in the writeup and also believed this result to be implicitly known in the Pollack-Pomerance-Treviño paper (the one cited in the writeup).

A slightly stronger statement follows from the usual shifted-collision estimates for the totient function. Let \(c>0\) be fixed and put \(L=\lfloor(\log x)^c\rfloor\). I will use the following standard consequence of the Erdős--Pomerance--Sárközy/Graham--Holt--Pomerance primitive-set method: for every \(A,B>0\), uniformly for \(1\le h\le(\log X)^B\), one has\[ R_h(X):=\#\{m\le X:\varphi(m)=\varphi(m+h)\}\ll_{A,B}\frac{X}{(\log X)^A}. \]This is unconditional. Call \(n\le x-L\) bad if the values \(\varphi(n+1),\ldots,\varphi(n+L)\) are not all distinct. If \(n\) is bad, then there exist \(1\le i<j\le L\) such that \(\varphi(n+i)=\varphi(n+j)\). Writing \(h=j-i\) and \(m=n+i\), this gives \(\varphi(m)=\varphi(m+h)\), with \(1\le h\le L\) and \(m\le x+L\). For fixed \(h\), there are at most \(L-h\le L\) possible values of \(i\). Hence the number of bad \(n\le x-L\) is at most\[ \sum_{h=1}^{L-1}(L-h)R_h(x+L)\le L\sum_{h=1}^{L}R_h(x+L). \]Taking \(B>c\) in the shifted-collision estimate gives, for every \(A>0\),\[ R_h(x+L)\ll_{A,c}\frac{x}{(\log x)^A} \]uniformly for \(1\le h\le L\). Therefore\[ \#\{n\le x-L:n\text{ is bad}\}\ll_{A,c}L^2\frac{x}{(\log x)^A}\ll_{A,c}x(\log x)^{2c-A}. \]Choosing \(A>2c+1\), this is \(o(x)\). Consequently almost every \(n\le x\) has the property that \(\varphi(n+1),\ldots,\varphi(n+\lfloor(\log x)^c\rfloor)\) are pairwise distinct. In particular, for all sufficiently large \(x\), there exists some \(n\le x\) such that the values \(\varphi(n+k)\), \(1\le k\le(\log x)^c\), are all distinct.
Do you have a reference for "the following standard consequence of the Erdős--Pomerance--Sárközy/Graham--Holt--Pomerance primitive-set method"? I'm not sure it's true.

Yes Chojecki's objection is correct: the displayed estimate\[ R_h(X)\ll_{A,B} X(\log X)^{-A} \]uniformly for \(1\le h\le(\log X)^B\) does not seem to be a standard consequence of the Erdős--Pomerance--Sárközy/Graham--Holt--Pomerance method. What is known is more like a decomposition into the structured part \(P_0(x;h)\), of size \(\ll c(h)x/(\log x)^2\), and an exceptional part \(P_1(x;h)\), for which one does get much stronger exponential-log savings uniformly in \(h\). The structured part is precisely the obstruction to obtaining arbitrary powers of \(\log x\). Thus the union-bound argument after the estimate is fine, but the quoted estimate itself is too strong as stated. With the known \(x/(\log x)^2\)-type uniform bound one only gets a much more limited polylogarithmic range, not the claimed statement for every fixed \(c>0\). Happy to remove if community deems it better to be scrubbed.
Standard check, like Chojecki, did not believe that the standard consequence step is correct. I'd suggest to supply further reference if you believe it's correct.
The correct spelling for “Pomerenace” should be “Pomerance”. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
